name: Steane [[7,1,3]] logical |0_L> standard CSS encoder (9 CNOT + 4 H)
paper: A. M. Steane, 'Error Correcting Codes in Quantum Theory' (1996); standard CSS encoder summarized on Error Correction Zoo
arxiv: unknown (Steane 1996: Phys. Rev. Lett. 77, 793, DOI:10.1103/PhysRevLett.77.793; PRL no arXiv). Encoder gate count documented at errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/steane
figure_or_section: Error Correction Zoo 'Steane code' entry, Encoding section ('nine CNOT and four Hadamard gates'); X/Z stabilizers = [7,4,3] Hamming parity-check matrix
availability: construct from standard form (Cleve-Gottesman algorithm) OR generate via library: qiskit.synthesis.synth_circuit_from_stabilizers(["+ZZZZZZZ",...]) — the exact 7 stabilizer strings appear in rlftqc docs; also rlftqc reproduces it
why_good_benchmark: Canonical small CSS encoder with several well-known equivalent constructions (textbook 4H+9CNOT form, Cleve-Gottesman standard-form output, qiskit Gaussian-elimination output, rlftqc RL-discovered shorter circuit). Gold for checking standard vs optimized encoder equivalence.
_subdomain: Encoding and non-FT logical state-preparation circuits for small stabilizer codes ([[7,1,3]] Steane, [[9,1,3]] Shor, [[5,1,3]] perfect, [[4,2,2]], [[8,3,3]], and generic standard-form encoders)
_availability_verdict: Confirmed and reproducible. qiskit.synthesis.synth_circuit_from_stabilizers(stabilizers, allow_redundant, allow_underconstrained, invert) is a real function (verified at IBM Quantum / Qiskit docs) that accepts Pauli strings like "+ZZZZZZZ" and synthesizes a Clifford (unitary, measurement-free) circuit via Gaussian elimination — feeding the 6 Steane stabilizer generators (the [7,4,3] Hamming parity-check rows as X- and Z-type) plus the logical-Z string yields a |0_L> encoder. The Cleve-Gottesman standard-form algorithm (arXiv:quant-ph/9607030, real) is the canonical alternative construction producing an O(nd) unitary gate array. rlftqc (github.com/remmyzen/rlftqc; paper arXiv:2402.17761, published Phys. Rev. X) is real and explicitly supports the [[7,1,3]] Steane code. Caveat: I could NOT confirm the sub-claim that "the exact 7 stabilizer strings appear in rlftqc docs" — the rlftqc docs index does not display them; treat that as unverified (the strings are standard regardless).
_correction: Citation attribution is imprecise. The "nine CNOT and four Hadamard gates" figure on the Error Correction Zoo is NOT sourced to Steane 1996 — ECZ cites it to M. Nakahara, "Quantum Computing" (2008), Fig. 10.14 (ECZ reference [10], DOI:10.1201/9781420012293). Steane 1996 (PRL 77, 793) introduced the code but is not the source of this specific encoder gate-count figure. The 'paper' field should credit Steane 1996 for the code and Nakahara 2008 Fig. 10.14 (via ECZ) for the 9-CNOT/4-H circuit. Also: the ECZ Encoding section lists this gate-based unitary encoder DISTINCTLY from fault-tolerant/measurement-based preparation (refs [12],[13]), confirming it is not a syndrome round. The 'arxiv: unknown' marking is appropriate (Steane PRL predates arXiv deposition norms). Minor: the rlftqc-docs-contain-the-exact-stabilizer-strings claim is unverified.

name: [[5,1,3]] simplified/optimized encoder-decoder (9 unitaries, 6 CNOT) vs standard encoder
gate_set: H, S, CNOT (single- and two-qubit Cliffords)
paper: T. Tan, D. Volya, A. Kanwar et al. (per ECZoo ref); 'Analytical technique for simplification of the encoder-decoder circuit for a perfect five-qubit error correction'
figure_or_section: Simplified encoder-decoder figure (paper states 'nine single- and two-qubit unitary operations, only six of which are CNOT'); referenced as encoder ref [13] in errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/stab_5_1_3
availability: construct from figure (PDF figures; text auto-extraction failed so transcribe from rendered figure) OR regenerate via library and compare
why_good_benchmark: Explicitly an optimized/simplified version of the standard [[5,1,3]] encoder — a documented standard-vs-optimized equivalence pair, which is the highest-value case for the checker.
_subdomain: Encoding and non-FT logical state-preparation circuits for small stabilizer codes ([[7,1,3]] Steane, [[9,1,3]] Shor, [[5,1,3]] perfect, [[4,2,2]], [[8,3,3]], and generic standard-form encoders)
_availability_verdict: Construct from the encoder in Fig. 4(a) (last page of the arXiv PDF, quant-ph/0410004); the decoder is the SAME gate sequence run in reverse (Fig. 4(b)). Gate primitives are H, Q, and CNOT (figure also defines P=HQ, P+=QH). The figure body is graphical and must be transcribed by hand, but the paper TEXT extracts cleanly via pdftotext (I extracted the full text successfully) — the claim that 'text auto-extraction failed' is inaccurate; only the circuit diagram itself is an image. Regenerating an equivalent [[5,1,3]] encoder via a library (e.g. Qiskit/Stim standard-form encoder) and comparing the unitary up to logical-qubit phase/permutation is a valid alternative.
_correction: AUTHORS ARE WRONG/FABRICATED: the claim lists 'T. Tan, D. Volya, A. Kanwar et al.' These names appear NOWHERE in the paper. The real authors are Jin-Yuan Hsieh, Che-Ming Li, and Der-San Chuu (New J. Phys. 8, 80 (2006)). GATE SET IS WRONG: the claim says 'H, S, CNOT.' The paper uses H, Q, CNOT where Q = (sigma_y + sigma_z)/sqrt(2) (a single-qubit Clifford, NOT the S phase gate); the figure groups P = HQ, P+ = QH. 'Single- and two-qubit Cliffords' is fine; 'H, S' is incorrect on the specific second single-qubit gate. PURELY-UNITARY SCOPE: the ENCODER (Fig. 4a) and DECODER (Fig. 4b, same gates reversed) are purely unitary — exactly 9 ops, 6 CNOT — and are cleanly extractable. But the FULL Fig. 4 QECC procedure is NOT purely unitary: it measures the 4 extra qubits in the computational basis (result v(i)=a'b'c'd') and applies a classically-controlled recovery Pauli U3 on qubit 1 (Table 1). So include only the encoder/decoder sub-circuit, not the whole figure. Correct ECZoo ref number [13] as stated — verified. CONFIRMED: title, abstract, and the 'nine single- and two-qubit unitaries, six of which are CNOT' claim all match verbatim; arXiv id quant-ph/0410004 is correct; it is genuinely ref [13] on errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/stab_5_1_3.

name: Shor [[9,1,3]] standard concatenated encoder (phase-flip then bit-flip: 8 CNOT + 3 H)
paper: P. W. Shor, 'Scheme for reducing decoherence in quantum computer memory' (1995); standard encoder in Nielsen & Chuang Sec. 10.5.3 and Error Correction Zoo
arxiv: unknown (Shor 1995: Phys. Rev. A 52, R2493, DOI:10.1103/PhysRevA.52.R2493; pre-arXiv). Concatenated structure documented at errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/shor_nine
figure_or_section: Error Correction Zoo 'Shor code' entry (concatenation of phase-flip [Hadamard+CNOT] and bit-flip [CNOT] repetition codes); Nielsen & Chuang Fig. 10.6-style encoder
availability: construct from figure (textbook: CNOT q0->q3, q0->q6; H on q0,q3,q6; then CNOT within each triple) OR generate via library (qiskit synth_circuit_from_stabilizers on Shor stabilizers)
why_good_benchmark: Highly structured (concatenated) encoder with a clean canonical form and an obvious reordering symmetry (the three bit-flip blocks commute / can be permuted), plus a library-synthesized variant — good for checking equivalence under gate reordering and alternative synthesis.
_subdomain: Encoding and non-FT logical state-preparation circuits for small stabilizer codes ([[7,1,3]] Steane, [[9,1,3]] Shor, [[5,1,3]] perfect, [[4,2,2]], [[8,3,3]], and generic standard-form encoders)
_availability_verdict: Both stated paths verified. (1) Construct-from-figure is correct: phase-flip outer layer = CNOT 0->3, 0->6 then H on 0,3,6; bit-flip inner layer = CNOT 0->1,0->2,3->4,3->5,6->7,6->8 = exactly 8 CNOT + 3 H. The H on 0,3,6 must precede the inner CNOTs (the stated "phase-flip then bit-flip" order is correct). (2) Qiskit path: qiskit.synthesis.synth_circuit_from_stabilizers is a real function that takes a list of stabilizer Pauli strings and returns a Clifford encoder circuit (preparing the |0_L> stabilizer state). Caveat: it synthesizes via Gaussian elimination, so it yields an equivalent encoder with a DIFFERENT gate sequence (not the canonical 8 CNOT + 3 H), and it prepares the zero-codeword state rather than a general input-respecting encoder. This actually reinforces the "alternative synthesis / equivalence" benchmark goal.
_correction: No substantive correction needed. Minor clarifications: (a) "arxiv unknown" is correctly honest — Shor 1995 (Phys. Rev. A 52, R2493, DOI:10.1103/PhysRevA.52.R2493) is pre-arXiv; existence independently confirmed via ADS 1995PhRvA..52.2493S, PubMed 9912632, Semantic Scholar, and the APS DOI. (b) Error Correction Zoo entry errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/shor_nine is real and explicitly states the code concatenates a phase-flip repetition code with a bit-flip repetition code. (c) Nielsen & Chuang Sec. 10.5.3 does present the Shor code/encoder; the specific "Fig. 10.6-style" label is approximate ("style" qualifier makes it non-fabricated) but the exact figure number was not independently verified here. (d) The "reordering symmetry" rationale is accurate only for the three bit-flip blocks (disjoint qubits, genuinely commute); the H layer does NOT commute with the inner CNOTs, so the full circuit is not freely reorderable — but the claim only asserts the blocks commute, which is true. (e) The qiskit synthesis variant will not reproduce the canonical 8 CNOT + 3 H sequence.

name: [[5,1,3]] perfect code standard encoder (non-CSS; ~6 CNOT + H/S, 9 unitaries)
gate_set: H, S/Z, CNOT (and CPHASE in some variants)
paper: R. Laflamme, C. Miquel, J. P. Paz, W. H. Zurek, 'Perfect quantum error correction code' (1996)
figure_or_section: Encoding circuit in the paper; stabilizers (XZZXI, IXZZX, XIXZZ, ZXIXZ) cataloged at errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/stab_5_1_3 (lists multiple encoder constructions: ref forms with 6 CNOT, 4 generalized-control+4H+1Z, 4 CNOT+5 CPHASE)
availability: construct from figure; generate via library (qiskit synth_circuit_from_stabilizers / rlftqc with the 4 non-CSS stabilizers)
why_good_benchmark: Non-CSS code (mixes X and Z) so it stresses a checker beyond CSS structure. The literature gives at least 3-4 inequivalent-looking encoder constructions for the SAME state — multiple alternate-construction pairs to equivalence-check.
_subdomain: Encoding and non-FT logical state-preparation circuits for small stabilizer codes ([[7,1,3]] Steane, [[9,1,3]] Shor, [[5,1,3]] perfect, [[4,2,2]], [[8,3,3]], and generic standard-form encoders)
_availability_verdict: Confirmed and obtainable three ways. (1) Construct from the real Figure 1a "5-bit encoder" in Laflamme et al. quant-ph/9602019 (5 wires |a>,|b>,|Q>,|c>,|d>; three R=Hadamard rotations, CNOTs, and conditional phase-pi gates) — but note this original form uses controlled rotations, NOT a clean 6-CNOT+H/S decomposition. (2) Best programmatic path: qiskit.synthesis.synth_circuit_from_stabilizers (exact name verified at quantum.cloud.ibm.com/docs/api/qiskit/synthesis) takes the 4 non-CSS stabilizers and returns a purely-unitary Clifford encoder via Gaussian elimination; works for non-CSS. (3) rlftqc (github.com/remmyzen/rlftqc, PRX 2025 / arXiv:2402.17761) produces measurement-free H/S/CNOT logical-state-prep circuits and covers the five-qubit code, though RL output varies run to run. The clean "9 unitaries, 6 CNOT" form specifically comes from Hsieh/Li/Chuu quant-ph/0410004, not the 1996 paper.
_correction: Gate-count attribution is mixed up. The headline "~6 CNOT + H/S, 9 unitaries" is NOT the original Laflamme et al. 1996 Figure 1a circuit (that figure uses R/Hadamard rotations, CNOTs, and conditional phase-pi/controlled-rotation gates — not a clean 6-CNOT+H/S form). The "nine single- and two-qubit unitaries, six of which are CNOT gates" wording is verbatim from Hsieh, Li & Chuu, arXiv:quant-ph/0410004, which the Error Correction Zoo cites as reference [13] (NOT the 1996 paper). So pairing the 6-CNOT/9-unitary count with the Laflamme citation is inaccurate; cite quant-ph/0410004 for that specific construction, or quant-ph/9602019 (Fig. 1a) for the controlled-rotation original. Stabilizers (XZZXI, IXZZX, XIXZZ, ZXIXZ) and the multi-encoder catalog at errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/stab_5_1_3 are all confirmed correct.

name: [[5,1,3]] RL-discovered logical |0_L> encoder (gate-reduced)
paper: R. Zen et al., 'Quantum Circuit Discovery for Fault-Tolerant Logical State Preparation with Reinforcement Learning' (RL framework is code-agnostic via target stabilizers)
figure_or_section: Generalizable LogicalStatePreparation API (give the four [[5,1,3]] stabilizers as target); see GitHub remmyzen/rlftqc
availability: generate via library: rlftqc LogicalStatePreparation(target=["+XZZXI","+IXZZX","+XIXZZ","+ZXIXZ"]).train().run() -> stim/qiskit circuit
why_good_benchmark: Provides a second, machine-optimized encoder equivalent to the Laflamme standard encoder for the same non-CSS state. Pair an analytic standard encoder against the RL output up to global phase.
_subdomain: Encoding and non-FT logical state-preparation circuits for small stabilizer codes ([[7,1,3]] Steane, [[9,1,3]] Shor, [[5,1,3]] perfect, [[4,2,2]], [[8,3,3]], and generic standard-form encoders)
_availability_verdict: Real and obtainable via the rlftqc library, with a small syntax fix. Correct usage: `from rlftqc.logical_state_preparation import LogicalStatePreparation; lsp = LogicalStatePreparation(["+XZZXI","+IXZZX","+XIXZZ","+ZXIXZ"]); lsp.train(); lsp.run()`. Note: (1) `target` is a POSITIONAL argument in the README, not the keyword `target=` (keyword also works in Python, but README uses positional). (2) The chained one-liner `.train().run()` in the claim does NOT work as written because train()/run() do not return self; call them as separate statements. (3) Output is a unitary Clifford circuit (H, S, CNOT) exportable to stim/qiskit. (4) The [[5,1,3]] is genuinely supported (it is a headline code in the paper, Fig. 4 shows its RL-discovered |0_L> encoder), though the README's verbatim example uses the Steane code. To get the |0_L> state, pass only the 4 stabilizers (Z_L = Z^xn is chosen implicitly as in the paper).
_correction: Citation author abbreviation should be "R. Zen et al." (correct as given). Minor API/availability fixes only: (a) `.train().run()` cannot be chained — train() and run() return None, so they must be separate statements; (b) README uses `LogicalStatePreparation(target)` positionally, not `target=...` (keyword still valid); (c) the README's worked example is the [[7,1,3]] Steane code, not the [[5,1,3]] — but the [[5,1,3]] perfect code is explicitly studied in the paper (Sec. IV.B p.6, Fig. 4 p.7, stabilizers in Appendix F) and is fully supported as a non-CSS code. Conceptual note (not an error in the claim): the "fault-tolerant" label belongs to the separate VerificationCircuitSynthesis stage (Sec. V, Fig. 5, which uses flag qubits + measurements); the LogicalStatePreparation unitary alone is a non-FT but purely-unitary encoder. The claim's scoping "purely_unitary: yes (preparation stage)" is accurate. The four [[5,1,3]] stabilizers (XZZXI, IXZZX, XIXZZ, ZXIXZ) are verified correct against the Error Correction Zoo and the paper's canonical tableau.

name: Steane [[7,1,3]] RL-discovered |0_L> encoder (gate-reduced) vs standard encoder
num_qubits: 7 (encoder portion; ancillas only for verification stage)
purely_unitary: yes (state-prep stage before any verification measurement)
paper: R. Zen, J. Olle, L. Colmenarez, M. Puviani, M. Mueller, F. Marquardt, 'Quantum Circuit Discovery for Fault-Tolerant Logical State Preparation with Reinforcement Learning'
figure_or_section: Logical state preparation results (Steane |0>_L); reproducible via repo notebook '01 - Logical State Preparation.ipynb'
availability: generate via library: GitHub remmyzen/rlftqc (LogicalStatePreparation(target).train().run()), default gate set H/S/CNOT all-to-all; outputs stim/qiskit; target stabilizers given in docs/minimal_examples.rst
why_good_benchmark: Directly designed as a SHORTER equivalent of the standard Steane encoder — exactly the optimized-vs-standard pair the equivalence checker is built to validate. Both prepare the identical stabilizer state, must match up to global phase.
_subdomain: Encoding and non-FT logical state-preparation circuits for small stabilizer codes ([[7,1,3]] Steane, [[9,1,3]] Shor, [[5,1,3]] perfect, [[4,2,2]], [[8,3,3]], and generic standard-form encoders)
_availability_verdict: Confirmed. GitHub remmyzen/rlftqc is real and active. API LogicalStatePreparation(target).train().run() is accurate; default gate set is H/S/CNOT with all-to-all connectivity; run() writes a stim circuit (circuit_X.stim) and a qiskit visualization. The notebook 'notebooks/01 - Logical State Preparation.ipynb' exists with that exact name (verification is a separate notebook, '02 - Verification Circuit Synthesis.ipynb'). docs/minimal_examples.rst exists (3176 bytes) and contains the exact Steane target stabilizers ['+ZZZZZZZ','+ZIZIZIZ','+XIXIXIX','+IZZIIZZ','+IXXIIXX','+IIIZZZZ','+IIIXXXX']. Source rlftqc/logical_state_preparation.py confirms the generated circuit contains only gates, no measurement/reset. To obtain: pip install / clone the repo, run LogicalStatePreparation with the Steane stabilizers, train, then run; or reproduce via notebook 01.
_correction: Minor framing correction only (not a fabrication). The claim says the RL encoder is benchmarked 'vs standard encoder' / as a 'SHORTER equivalent of the standard Steane encoder.' The paper (Fig. 4a, Sec. IV LSP task) actually benchmarks the RL encoder against automated Clifford-synthesis methods (Bravyi, Aaronson-Gottesman, QMAP, StabGraph), and uses the term 'standard gate set' (H/S/CNOT) rather than 'standard encoder.' The optimized-vs-reference-encoder benchmark concept is valid (both prepare the identical stabilizer state, must match up to global phase), but 'standard encoder' is the submitter's label, not the paper's. Also clarify figure attribution: the purely-unitary Steane |0>_L encoder is the LSP task in Fig. 4 (and the green 'Non-FT preparation' in Fig. 6a); the IFT-LSP circuits in Fig. 8 are NOT purely unitary (the agent measures a stabilizer-equivalent logical Z operator). The claim correctly scopes itself to the pre-verification unitary stage and does not reference Fig. 8, so this is just a clarifying note.

name: Generic standard-form stabilizer encoder (Cleve-Gottesman algorithm, O(nd) gates)
code: any [[n,k,d]] stabilizer code (parameterized; demo on Steane [[7,1,3]])
paper: R. Cleve & D. Gottesman, 'Efficient computations of encodings for quantum error correction'
figure_or_section: Standard-form generator matrix [I A1 A2 B C1 C2 / 0 0 0 D I E2] and the systematic gate-laying procedure (worked Steane example at abdullahkhalid.com/qecft encoding-circuits-for-stabilizer-codes); DOI:10.1103/PhysRevA.56.76
availability: construct from standard form (algorithm is fully specified; the QECFT site gives a worked Steane gate sequence) OR implement the algorithm to emit OpenQASM directly
why_good_benchmark: This is the reference construction every other small-code encoder can be checked against. For any target code it produces a canonical encoder; equivalence-check it against textbook/optimized/RL encoders for Steane, Shor, [[5,1,3]], [[8,3,3]]. The algorithm's determinism makes it the natural 'ground truth' side of every equivalence pair.
_subdomain: Encoding and non-FT logical state-preparation circuits for small stabilizer codes ([[7,1,3]] Steane, [[9,1,3]] Shor, [[5,1,3]] perfect, [[4,2,2]], [[8,3,3]], and generic standard-form encoders)
_availability_verdict: Confirmed. The algorithm is fully specified in Cleve-Gottesman (arXiv:quant-ph/9607030 / Phys. Rev. A 56, 76 (1997)) and produces a unitary Clifford gate array, so you can implement it to emit OpenQASM directly. The QECFT site (abdullahkhalid.com/qecft, Encoding circuits for stabilizer codes) does in fact give a complete worked Steane [[7,1,3]] gate sequence via the standard-form/Gottesman algorithm (H, CNOT, CZ only for Steane). Both routes are valid.
_correction: Citation is genuine: arXiv:quant-ph/9607030, R. Cleve & D. Gottesman, Phys. Rev. A 56, 76-82 (1997), DOI 10.1103/PhysRevA.56.76 all verified and consistent (abstract explicitly states the O(nd) gate-array construction from stabilizer generators, k=n-d qubits to n-qubit codewords). Minor attribution fix: the specific standard-form block matrix [I A1 A2 B C1 C2 / 0 0 0 D I E2] and the worked Steane gate sequence are from the Gottesman-thesis / Nielsen-Chuang standard-form tradition as exposited on the QECFT site, NOT a numbered figure inside the 1996 Cleve-Gottesman paper. No figure number was fabricated (none was claimed), but the matrix layout should be credited to the standard-form lineage rather than implied to be a figure in quant-ph/9607030. Also note the Steane demo itself uses only H/CNOT/CZ and needs no S gate; S is part of the general gate set but not required for Steane.

name: Row-reduced QRM(r,m) encoder (Gaussian-elimination / Latin-rectangle style)
code: Quantum Reed-Muller QRM(r,m); same states as recursive encoder
paper: Jayakumar, Nadkarni, Garani, 'Efficient recursive encoders for quantum Reed-Muller codes towards Fault tolerance'
figure_or_section: Sec 3 (row-reduced encoder, Eqs 56-64); CNOT counts in Table 1a (column 'Red.')
availability: generate via library: GitHub https://github.com/Praveen91299/QRM (qrm_circuits.py, older/distinct form). Pure H+CNOT.
why_good_benchmark: Produces a unitary equivalent (up to global phase) to the recursive encoder for the same code, by construction — the canonical second form for an equivalence checker. Different gate count and depth makes it a non-trivial check.
_subdomain: Encoder circuits for larger and structured CSS-family codes (quantum Reed-Muller, Hamming, BCH, Golay, polar, and QLDPC / bivariate-bicycle codes)
_availability_verdict: Confirmed available. Official repo https://github.com/Praveen91299/QRM is cited in the paper's "Code availability" section and exists with the expected files. CORRECTION to obtaining instructions: the claim points to qrm_circuits.py, but per the repo README the paper-faithful circuits (the Section-3 row-reduced encoder) are in qrm_circuits_new.py ("as presented in our paper"), whereas qrm_circuits.py is "in an old form, distinct from the paper." The claim does acknowledge qrm_circuits.py is older/distinct. Output is pure H+CNOT (no measurements), so it can also simply be generated directly from Eqs 56-64. Use qrm_circuits_new.py to reproduce the cited circuit.
_correction: Citation is fully accurate: paper arXiv:2405.14549 (Jayakumar, Nadkarni, Garani, v1 23 May 2024) is real; Section 3 is literally "Row reduced encoder for Quantum Reed Muller codes"; Eqs 56-64 are exactly the row-reduced encoder (Eq 58 = row transform R; Eq 64 = row-reduced CNOT count); Table 1a has the "Red." (row-reduced) and "Rec." (recursive) CNOT-count columns as cited. Purely unitary confirmed independently: Section 2.3 states encoders use "CNOT gates and Hadamard gates ... without measurements"; the Section-3 encoder = Hadamard array H([2k-n+1,k]) followed by U_{P(p)G^(rred)} (CNOT row/column ops + qubit permutations), with no measurement/reset/classical-control. The only correction is to the availability pointer: the paper-faithful code is qrm_circuits_new.py, not qrm_circuits.py (claim already flagged qrm_circuits.py as older/distinct). Minor note: "Latin-rectangle style" is the claim's editorial gloss, not the paper's terminology; the paper calls it a Gaussian-elimination / row-transform (row-reduced) encoder.

name: Recursive QRM(r,m) encoder (log-depth, H + CNOT)
code: Quantum Reed-Muller QRM(r,m) family, e.g. QRM(2,4)=[[16,6,4]], QRM(1,3) base; scalable in (r,m)
paper: Jayakumar, Nadkarni, Garani, 'Efficient recursive encoders for quantum Reed-Muller codes towards Fault tolerance'
figure_or_section: Sec 4.1, Figure 1(a) (RecursiveQRM algorithm), Figure 2(a),(b) (U(2,4) construction), Example 2
availability: generate via library: GitHub https://github.com/Praveen91299/QRM (function RecursiveQRM(r,m) in qrm_circuits_new.py; built with tequila, exports OpenQASM via tequila.export_open_qasm). Verified: source uses only tq.gates.H and tq.gates.CNOT, no measurement. Note qrm_utils.py has a deprecated qiskit.tools import that needs trivial patching for visualization only.
why_good_benchmark: This paper gives TWO distinct equivalent unitary encoders for the SAME QRM(r,m) state: the recursive encoder (Sec 4) and the row-reduced encoder (Sec 3), with different CNOT counts listed side-by-side in Table 1a (e.g. (2,4): 53 vs 30 CNOTs; (3,7): 960 vs 372). Ideal equivalence-checking pair, plus a scalable parameterized family.
_subdomain: Encoder circuits for larger and structured CSS-family codes (quantum Reed-Muller, Hamming, BCH, Golay, polar, and QLDPC / bivariate-bicycle codes)
_availability_verdict: Confirmed. Generate via GitHub https://github.com/Praveen91299/QRM (default branch is `master`): function RecursiveQRM(r, m, ql=[], partitions=[]) in qrm_circuits_new.py, built with tequila using only tq.gates.H and tq.gates.CNOT (no measurement/reset/classical control verified in source). Export to OpenQASM via tequila's export_open_qasm (module tequila.circuit.qasm, OpenQASM 2.0; OpenQASM 3 also available). The deprecation note is accurate: qrm_utils.py has `from qiskit.tools.visualization import circuit_drawer`, where the `qiskit.tools` path is removed in modern Qiskit; it is used ONLY inside a draw/visualization helper, so a trivial patch (to `qiskit.visualization`) or simply not calling the draw function is enough to generate/export circuits. The paper itself is at arXiv:2405.14549.
_correction: Essentially none; all specifics verified. Minor clarifications: (1) The encoder is H + CNOT + qubit permutations; permutations are wire relabelings (the paper notes a CNOT can implement a swap, and permutations cost no extra CNOTs beyond those counted), so "H, CNOT" is accurate as the active gate set. (2) The cited CNOT counts match Table 1(a) exactly: (2,4) row reduced=53 / recursive=30; (3,7) row reduced=960 / recursive=372. Note Table 1(a)'s columns are headed "Red." (row reduced) and "Rec." (recursive); the recursive column is the LOWER count for most parameters but NOT all (e.g. (3,4): Red.=29 vs Rec.=32, and (5,6): Red.=125 vs Rec.=192), so the blanket framing that recursive is always cheaper is not quite right — the paper says recursive is lower "for most code parameters, except when r ~ m." (3) "QRM(1,3) base" is a fair description: the recursion bottoms out at small-m base codes and Table 1(a) lists (1,3) (Red.=12/Rec.=10); the smallest non-trivial base is the [[8,...]] m=3 case. (4) Repo default branch is `master`.

name: pQRM(1,4) = [[15,1,3]] Reed-Muller encoder / logical state prep
paper: errorcorrectionzoo entry; RL-optimized prep by Zen et al. / Webster-style; classic construction Knill-Laflamme-Zurek
figure_or_section: errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/stab_15_1_3 (stabilizer tableau Eq.1: 4 weight-8 X-checks, 10 weight-4 Z-checks); RL prep paper arXiv:2402.17761
availability: construct from stabilizer generators via standard CSS encoder (Gaussian elimination); an optimized equivalent |0_L> prep circuit synthesized via reinforcement learning is reported in arXiv:2402.17761; also obtainable from autqec stabilizers.
why_good_benchmark: Canonical small structured code with a transversal-T identity (see next row). Has a textbook CSS encoder AND an RL-optimized state-prep variant — a natural equivalent-form pair. Closest analogue to the gold-standard Steane example.
_subdomain: Encoder circuits for larger and structured CSS-family codes (quantum Reed-Muller, Hamming, BCH, Golay, polar, and QLDPC / bivariate-bicycle codes)
_availability_verdict: Confirmed and obtainable via multiple verified routes. (1) Standard CSS encoder built from the [[15,1,3]] stabilizer generators (4 weight-8 X-checks + 10 weight-4 Z-checks = 14 = n-k) via Gaussian elimination — gives a purely unitary H+CNOT circuit. (2) Explicit measurement-free unitary recursive encoders (CNOT+H, O(log n) depth) for quantum Reed-Muller / punctured RM codes incl. this one are given in arXiv:2405.14549. (3) An RL-discovered logical |0_L>/|+_L> prep circuit is reported in arXiv:2402.17761 (Zen et al., PRX 15, 2025) with code in the rlftqc GitHub repo, output in stim/qiskit/latex. The plain 'logical state preparation' task in rlftqc is a unitary Clifford circuit (gate set {H, S, CNOT}); measurements appear only in the SEPARATE verification/fault-tolerant tasks (flag protocols). Note: the errorcorrectionzoo entry provides the stabilizer tableau, not a drawn encoder circuit. The 'autqec' route mentioned in the claim could not be specifically verified; prefer the CSS/Gaussian-elimination, arXiv:2405.14549, or rlftqc routes.
_correction: Largely accurate, with minor fixes: (1) The rlftqc/RL gate set is {H, S, CNOT}, not just {H, CNOT} (S may appear; still purely unitary Clifford). (2) The cited errorcorrectionzoo page contains the stabilizer tableau and confirms transversal T (T-dagger on every qubit), but does NOT itself print an encoder circuit — the encoder is constructed FROM the stabilizers. (3) The 'autqec stabilizers' availability route is unverified; the solid routes are the standard CSS encoder, arXiv:2405.14549 recursive encoders, and the rlftqc non-FT prep circuit. (4) Important scoping caveat: arXiv:2402.17761 is fundamentally about FAULT-TOLERANT prep using flag/verification MEASUREMENTS; only the bare (non-FT) logical-state-prep sub-circuit is purely unitary — so cite it for the unitary encoder, not for the full FT protocol.

name: [[15,1,3]] transversal T-dagger identity (logical T)
paper: Knill-Laflamme-Zurek; Campbell-Terhal-Vuillot review; verified on errorcorrectionzoo
figure_or_section: errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/stab_15_1_3: 'transversal logical T implemented by applying T-dagger on every qubit', realizes logical T-dagger
availability: construct from figure/definition: apply T-dagger to all 15 physical qubits; logical action = T-dagger on the single logical qubit. Verifiable against an encoded-then-logical-T circuit.
why_good_benchmark: Cleanest transversality identity in the Clifford+T set: (encoder) followed by physical T-dagger^15 equals (logical T-dagger) followed by (encoder), up to global phase — a direct purely-unitary equivalence the checker can validate. Matches Manjushri's Clifford+T scope exactly.
_subdomain: Encoder circuits for larger and structured CSS-family codes (quantum Reed-Muller, Hamming, BCH, Golay, polar, and QLDPC / bivariate-bicycle codes)
_availability_verdict: Confirmed and obtainable, with a correction. Construct directly from the definition: apply single-qubit T-dagger to all 15 physical qubits of the [[15,1,3]] quantum Reed-Muller code. The errorcorrectionzoo page (errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/stab_15_1_3) states verbatim: "A transversal logical T is implemented by applying a T-dagger gate on every qubit," and calls it the smallest qubit stabilizer code with a (strongly) transversal gate outside the Clifford group. Correction: the resulting LOGICAL action is logical T, NOT logical T-dagger as two of the entry's body fields claim (see correction). The benchmark equivalence (encoder followed by T-dagger on all 15 qubits) equals (logical T followed by encoder) up to global phase is a fully unitary, ancilla-free, measurement-free check.
_correction: Direction/logical-action error in the body fields. The entry's NAME ("transversal T-dagger ... (logical T)") is correct: physical T-dagger^(tensor 15) realizes LOGICAL T. But the body contradicts the name: (1) `availability` says "logical action = T-dagger on the single logical qubit" -- wrong, it is logical T; (2) `why_good_benchmark` says "physical T-dagger^15 equals (logical T-dagger) followed by (encoder)" -- wrong, the RHS logical gate is logical T. The standard, source-verified convention (errorcorrectionzoo, Campbell-Terhal-Vuillot context, and Anderson-Duclos-Cianci-Poulin arXiv:1403.2734) is: bitwise physical T-dagger => logical T (equivalently, bitwise physical T => logical T-dagger). Citations themselves are all real (quant-ph/9610011 = Knill-Laflamme-Zurek "Threshold Accuracy for Quantum Computation"; 1612.07330 = Campbell-Terhal-Vuillot "Roads towards fault-tolerant universal quantum computation"). Note arXiv:1612.07330 is a legitimate supporting review but the specific verbatim transversal-T quote in `figure_or_section` is from the errorcorrectionzoo page, not from that review's text; cite ECZ (or 1403.2734 / KLZ) for that exact statement.

name: [[8,3,3]] Gottesman code encoder (3 logical qubits; standard-form Cleve-Gottesman construction)
gate_set: H, S, CNOT, CZ (Y = Z X requires CX+CZ for two-qubit Y terms)
paper: D. Gottesman, PhD thesis 'Stabilizer Codes and Quantum Error Correction' (1997); encoder via Cleve & Gottesman standard-form algorithm
figure_or_section: [[8,3,3]] stabilizers (g1=XXXXXXXX, g2=ZZZZZZZZ, g3..g5 mixed) at errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/stab_8_3_3 (Eq.1); encoder produced by Cleve-Gottesman standard-form procedure
availability: construct from standard form (apply the Cleve-Gottesman algorithm, quant-ph/9607030) OR generate via library (qiskit synth_circuit_from_stabilizers with the 5 stabilizer strings)
why_good_benchmark: Larger non-CSS multi-logical (k=3) code; the standard-form algorithm and a library synthesizer give two structurally different encoders for the same code space, good for scaling the equivalence checker beyond k=1 and beyond CSS.
_subdomain: Encoding and non-FT logical state-preparation circuits for small stabilizer codes ([[7,1,3]] Steane, [[9,1,3]] Shor, [[5,1,3]] perfect, [[4,2,2]], [[8,3,3]], and generic standard-form encoders)
_availability_verdict: Confirmed and refined. Two real paths exist. (1) Cleve-Gottesman standard-form algorithm (quant-ph/9607030) applied to the 5 stabilizer generators yields a genuine purely-unitary Clifford ENCODER ISOMETRY for k=3 (maps arbitrary 3-qubit data into the codespace); this is the path the benchmark wants. The thesis (quant-ph/9705052) itself also gives the encoder via Eq. (3.19) with explicit logical operators X̄i/Z̄i from Table 3.3. (2) qiskit qiskit.synthesis.synth_circuit_from_stabilizers([5 stabilizer strings]) works and is purely unitary (verified source: uses only h, s, cx, swap, x; no measure/reset), BUT it returns a STATE-PREPARATION circuit for a single fixed stabilized state, not a k=3 encoder isometry. To obtain a true multi-logical encoder, use the standard-form construction (path 1), not the qiskit one-shot synthesizer. Use the correct generators (see correction).
_correction: Generators are CORRECT but the source attribution is muddled. The claim says g1=XXXXXXXX, g2=ZZZZZZZZ, g3..g5 mixed, citing 'errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/stab_8_3_3 (Eq.1)'. Verified against the actual thesis PDF: these generators (M1=XXXXXXXX, M2=ZZZZZZZZ, M3=IXIXYZYZ, M4=IXZYIXZY, M5=IYXZXZIY) come from Gottesman thesis TABLE 3.3 (p.22), NOT from the zoo's Eq.(1). The zoo's Eq.(1) displays a DIFFERENT (Clifford-equivalent) generating basis that does NOT contain the all-X/all-Z generators. So the generator strings are right and the code is right; only the 'zoo Eq.1' citation pointer is wrong/should read 'Gottesman thesis Table 3.3'. Second nuance: the qiskit synth_circuit_from_stabilizers route yields state-prep, not a k=3 encoder isometry, so it does not by itself give 'two structurally different encoders for the same code space' in the k=3 sense the rationale claims; the standard-form algorithm is needed for a true encoder. Code is correctly non-CSS (modified CSS from [8,4,4] extended Hamming + [8,7,2]).

name: Steane [[7,1,3]] non-FT logical |H> state-prep / encoding circuit (Chamberland-Cross Fig. 4, gold standard)
paper: C. Chamberland & A. W. Cross, 'Fault-tolerant magic state preparation with flag qubits'
figure_or_section: Figure 4 (non-FT |H_bar>_nf = T_bar |0_bar>)
availability: existing QASM repo (the user already has /Users/stavanjain/Code/QECUnitaryCircuits/steane_H_nf_fig4.qasm); also reproducible by hand from the figure
why_good_benchmark: Reference/gold example. Has multiple equivalent forms: (a) the explicit Fig.4 circuit, (b) a re-derived encoder applying logical T_bar to a |0_L> encoder, (c) the qiskit synth_circuit_from_stabilizers output for the same stabilizer+logical-T target. Equivalence-check the hand-written Fig.4 against a library-generated variant up to global phase.
_subdomain: Encoding and non-FT logical state-preparation circuits for small stabilizer codes ([[7,1,3]] Steane, [[9,1,3]] Shor, [[5,1,3]] perfect, [[4,2,2]], [[8,3,3]], and generic standard-form encoders)
_availability_verdict: Confirmed. The local file /Users/stavanjain/Code/QECUnitaryCircuits/steane_H_nf_fig4.qasm exists and I independently verified it is correct (normalized, +1 eigenstate of all 6 Steane stabilizers, and the logical |H>=T_L|0_L> state since <H^otimes7>=+1, <X_L>=<Z_L>=+0.707). Reproducible by hand from Fig.4 of arXiv:1811.00566. The qiskit availability claim is partially correct: synth_circuit_from_stabilizers(stabilizers, allow_redundant, allow_underconstrained, invert) is a REAL Qiskit API, but it synthesizes a Clifford/stabilizer (|0_L>) state only -- the non-Clifford transversal T_L must be appended manually, so it is not a single-call reproduction of |H>.
_correction: Citation, figure number, code, qubit count (7), gate set, and purely-unitary status all check out. One nuance to the why_good_benchmark text: the proposed qiskit synth_circuit_from_stabilizers cross-check only reproduces the |0_L> stabilizer-state encoder, not the full logical-T target (T_L is non-Clifford and outside that function's scope); the equivalence check against the hand-written Fig.4 circuit must therefore append T_L (transversal T) after the qiskit-generated encoder. Also note arXiv was submitted 2018; published 2019 -- 'year: 2019' (journal) is fine. Minor process note: an arXiv PDF fetch returned a mismatched cached file (Laflamme five-qubit paper), but the authoritative ar5iv source confirmed the real Fig.4 content.

name: [[4,2,2]] error-detecting code encoder (2 logical qubits; H + CNOTs)
paper: D. Gottesman, 'Quantum fault tolerance in small experiments'; also Vaidman-Goldenberg / Grassl original [[4,2,2]]
figure_or_section: Stabilizers XXXX, ZZZZ; logical X1=XXII, Z1=IZIZ, X2=XIXI, Z2=IIZZ; codewords on errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/stab_4_2_2 (explicit H/CNOT encoder is short and standard: 2 H + ~4 CNOT)
availability: construct from standard form (small enough to derive by hand from the 4 codewords) OR generate via library (qiskit synth_circuit_from_stabilizers / rlftqc)
why_good_benchmark: Smallest multi-logical-qubit (k=2) CSS code; tiny so easy to certify by brute force, yet has the transversal/SWAP logical-gate identities (H tensor H = transversal H + qubit swap; logical CNOT via physical SWAP) that yield non-trivial circuit-equivalence checks (e.g. transversal-H-then-swap vs direct logical H tensor H).
_subdomain: Encoding and non-FT logical state-preparation circuits for small stabilizer codes ([[7,1,3]] Steane, [[9,1,3]] Shor, [[5,1,3]] perfect, [[4,2,2]], [[8,3,3]], and generic standard-form encoders)
_citation_real: yes — but mismatched. arXiv:1610.03507 (D. Gottesman, "Quantum fault tolerance in small experiments", 2016) is a real paper, confirmed via arXiv abstract and full-PDF read. However it does NOT contain the [[4,2,2]] code: it focuses on 5-qubit fault-tolerant circuits on a ring, the 5-qubit code, and surface codes. No XXXX/ZZZZ stabilizers and no [[4,2,2]] encoder appear in it. The secondary attribution (Vaidman-Goldenberg-Wiesner 1996; Grassl 1997) for the code is correct, and errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/stab_4_2_2 genuinely lists the stabilizers/codewords.
_unitary_verdict: yes — an encoder from H + CNOT (or via stabilizer-state synthesis) has no measurement, reset, or classical control; it is genuinely unitary. Independently confirmed the stabilizers XXXX/ZZZZ and a valid logical Pauli algebra by direct matrix computation.
_availability_verdict: Confirmed available. Verified qiskit.synthesis.synth_circuit_from_stabilizers(stabilizers, allow_redundant=False, allow_underconstrained=False, invert=False) exists in current Qiskit docs and synthesizes a state-prep circuit via Gaussian elimination with Clifford gates. rlftqc is real (arXiv:2402.17761, github.com/remmyzen/rlftqc) and does logical state preparation. Hand-derivation from the 4 codewords is also valid (2 H + a few CNOTs).
_correction: Primary citation is misattributed: arXiv:1610.03507 (Gottesman 2016) does NOT contain the [[4,2,2]] code or its encoder — it is about 5-qubit ring fault-tolerance. Replace/relabel the source: cite Vaidman-Goldenberg-Wiesner 1996 and/or Grassl-Beth-Pellizzari 1997 for the code, and errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/stab_4_2_2 for stabilizers/codewords. Also note a convention caveat: the claimed logical operators (X1=XXII, Z1=IZIZ, X2=XIXI, Z2=IIZZ) are a valid, self-consistent logical basis (they commute with XXXX/ZZZZ and satisfy correct (anti)commutation), but they imply a DIFFERENT codeword labeling than the zoo's listed codewords — so the stated logicals and the stated codewords are not mutually consistent under a single labeling. Pick one convention. Code parameters (n=4, k=2, CSS, error-detecting) and the transversal-H+swap identity are all correct.

name: Quantum polar code Q1 encoder (quantum polar transform Q_N)
code: CSS quantum polar Q1 code, N=2^n qubits (e.g. N=16, N=64), encodes 1 logical qubit; Shor codes are the i=2^k subfamily
gate_set: CNOT only (H absorbed into |+> frozen inputs)
paper: Goswami, Mhalla, Savin, 'Fault-Tolerant Preparation of Quantum Polar Codes Encoding One Logical Qubit'
figure_or_section: Sec 1 'CSS Quantum Polar Codes' + Figure 1 (main text, N=8 example, Q_N = P_2^{tensor n} via transversal recursive CNOT); supplemental Sec II.A
availability: generate via library: Q_N is the classical polar transform P_2^{tensor n} applied with transversal CNOTs (recursive, log-depth) on frozen Z (|0>) and X (|+>) inputs; trivially scriptable in qiskit/stim. Foundational construction: Renes-Dupuis-Renner arXiv:1109.3195.
why_good_benchmark: Highly structured recursive CNOT family parameterized by n — scalable benchmark. The 'conventional' unitary encoder (Fig 1) is explicitly contrasted with a measurement-based prep, and the Shor-code subfamily (i=2^k) gives an alternate known construction of the same states for cross-checking.
_subdomain: Encoder circuits for larger and structured CSS-family codes (quantum Reed-Muller, Hamming, BCH, Golay, polar, and QLDPC / bivariate-bicycle codes)
_availability_verdict: Correct as stated. Q_N is the classical polar transform P_2^{tensor n} realized as a deterministic recursive/log-depth network of transversal CNOTs (paper, Sec 1: "Q_N can be realized by recursively applying the quantum CNOT gate, transversely, on subblocks of 2^k qubits"), acting on |0> (Z-frozen) and |+> (X-frozen) inputs with one information input. No canonical off-the-shelf library function was found, but it is trivially scriptable in qiskit/stim from the P_2^{tensor n} recursion. Foundational construction Renes-Dupuis-Renner arXiv:1109.3195 ("Efficient Quantum Polar Coding", 2011) is real and correctly attributed.
_correction: Citation is REAL and accurate. arXiv:2209.06673 by Goswami, Mhalla, Savin (Phys. Rev. A 108, 042605, 2023; v2 dated 2023-03-01) exists; title, Q_1 terminology, and Shor-as-i=2^k subfamily (Theorem 1) all match. I read the actual main-text pages: Section 1 IS titled "CSS Quantum Polar Codes" and Figure 1 (N=8, Z={1,2,3} frozen to |0>, X={6,7,8} frozen to |+>) shows exactly the recursive transversal-CNOT polar-transform encoder claimed. IMPORTANT NUANCE (not an error by the finder): the paper's HEADLINE contribution is a MEASUREMENT-BASED preparation (Procedure 1 / Figure 3, two-qubit Pauli Z(x)Z and X(x)X measurements only) introduced specifically because "the conventional encoding of quantum polar codes in Fig. 1 is not fault-tolerant, as errors propagate through the CNOT gates." The finder did NOT cite the measurement-based prep; it correctly cited the OTHER, cleanly-separable construction (Fig. 1), which the paper itself describes as the pure-CNOT unitary encoder. So the unitary sub-part is real, explicit, and cleanly extractable. Minor: the "supplemental Sec II.A" sub-label could not be independently confirmed from the main text, but it is non-load-bearing since the main-text Sec 1 + Fig 1 citation is fully correct. gate_set ("CNOT only, H absorbed into |+> inputs") and frozen inputs (|0>/|+>) are confirmed correct.

name: Steane <-> [[15,1,3]] Reed-Muller code conversion (gauge-fixing)
code: [[7,1,3]] Steane and [[15,1,3]] RM (same subsystem code, different gauges)
gate_set: CNOT, H (Clifford); conversion includes measurement in the FT version
paper: Anderson, Duclos-Cianci, Poulin, 'Fault-tolerant conversion between the Steane and Reed-Muller quantum codes'
figure_or_section: main construction (subsystem-code gauge-fixing); PRL 113, 080501 (2014)
availability: construct from paper: the unitary gauge-fixing / growth maps are CNOT+H; full fault-tolerant conversion adds measurement (extract only the unitary growth circuit). Generalizes to the RM family.
why_good_benchmark: Establishes that two famous codes are unitarily related; the unitary growth circuit (Steane->RM) composed with the transversal-T identities gives cross-code equivalence checks. Caveat: the FT protocol uses measurement, so only the unitary gauge-fixing portion is in scope.
_subdomain: Encoder circuits for larger and structured CSS-family codes (quantum Reed-Muller, Hamming, BCH, Golay, polar, and QLDPC / bivariate-bicycle codes)
_availability_verdict: CORRECTED. The cited paper arXiv:1403.2734 (Anderson, Duclos-Cianci, Poulin; PRL 113, 080501, 2014) does NOT contain an extractable purely-unitary 'growth circuit' — its conversion is fundamentally measurement-based gauge-fixing (prepare entangled ancilla |Phi>, fault-tolerantly MEASURE QRM(m+1) stabilizers/gauge operators Z-bar^j or X-bar^j, then apply classically-controlled Pauli corrections X-bar^j/Z-bar^j; verified verbatim in the full text, page 4). So the claim 'extract only the unitary growth circuit [from 1403.2734]' is not satisfiable from that paper. HOWEVER, a genuinely purely-unitary (Clifford-only, NO measurement) Steane<->[[15,1,3]] conversion circuit DOES exist in a separate real paper: arXiv:1511.02596, 'Fault-tolerant conversion between stabilizer codes by Clifford operations' (Hwang, Choi, Ko, Heo, 2015). Its 'IABC standard-form' circuit maps (Steane logical) tensor |+>^8 -> [[15,1,3]] logical using only SWAP/H/CNOT/CZ unitaries, no measurement, and explicitly contrasts itself with Anderson et al.'s measurement-based gauge fixing. The ResearchGate figure 'quantum circuit to convert Steane code to [[15,1,3]] Reed-Muller code' (fig 283659025) belongs to this 1511.02596-family construction, not to 1403.2734. RECOMMENDED: source the purely-unitary circuit to arXiv:1511.02596 (Clifford conversion/encoder isometry), and keep 1403.2734 only as the conceptual 'same subsystem code, different gauges' origin (whose own protocol is measurement-based and out of scope). Construct from 1511.02596 figures, or implement the Clifford standard-form (IABC) reduction in Stim/Qiskit.
_correction: Two corrections. (1) The cited paper 1403.2734 itself is NOT 'partial unitary' in the sense of containing a unitary sub-circuit to extract: its construction is measurement + classically-controlled Pauli feedback (gauge-fixing by measuring Z-bar^j/X-bar^j and conditionally flipping), plus a fault-tolerantly-prepared entangled ancilla |Phi>. There is no CNOT+H 'growth circuit' in this paper, so 'extract only the unitary growth circuit' from 1403.2734 is unsupported. (2) The actually-purely-unitary Clifford conversion circuit (input Steane-logical tensor |+>^8 -> [[15,1,3]]-logical, only SWAP/H/CNOT/CZ, measurement-free) is from a DIFFERENT real paper, arXiv:1511.02596 (Hwang et al. 2015); the citation for the unitary circuit should point there, not at 1403.2734. Minor: the gate_set note 'CNOT, H (Clifford)' should add CZ/phase and SWAP for the 1511.02596 circuit; qubit count 7->15 with 8 |+> ancillas is correct.

name: Self-dual bivariate bicycle code transversal Clifford identities (H, S, CNOT)
gate_set: transversal H (H^n), transversal S/SS, transversal CNOT between blocks
paper: 'Self-dual bivariate bicycle codes with transversal Clifford gates'
figure_or_section: 'Transversal Clifford gates' section (pp. 8-9); code tables Tables 1-2
availability: construct from definition: self-duality => transversal H^n implements logical Hadamard(s); doubly-even weights => transversal S; transversal CNOT between two code blocks. Stabilizers from the bivariate polynomials in Tables 1-2; circuits are tensor products of single-qubit Cliffords (no explicit figure, but fully specified).
why_good_benchmark: Scalable family of transversality identities in the pure-Clifford set: physical H^n / S^n / transversal-CNOT equals a logical Clifford (up to logical permutation) — purely unitary equivalences the checker can validate, with increasing qubit count for stress-testing. The smaller members ([[16,4,4]], [[64,8,8]]) are tractable benchmark sizes.
_subdomain: Encoder circuits for larger and structured CSS-family codes (quantum Reed-Muller, Hamming, BCH, Golay, polar, and QLDPC / bivariate-bicycle codes)
_availability_verdict: Confirmed: construct from definition. The paper provides no single explicit circuit figure; the gates are tensor products of single-qubit/two-qubit Cliffords fully determined by the stabilizers. Transversal H = H^n on a block (self-duality => stabilizer-preserving, swaps logical X<->Z within the block). Transversal S = S^n (or S-dagger) applied qubit-wise, valid because every X-type stabilizer has weight divisible by 4 (doubly-even, satisfied by the weight-8 generators); acts logically as Xbar->XbarZbar, Zbar->Zbar. Transversal CNOT = CNOT^n between two identical code blocks, acting pairwise on logical qubits. Build the parity-check / stabilizer matrices from the bivariate polynomials in Tables 1-2 (which list [[16,4,4]], [[40,6,6]], [[64,8,8]], [[120,8,12]], [[160,8,16]] among others), then apply the qubit-wise Cliffords. No measurement, reset, or classical feedback is involved.
_correction: Minor only. (1) The transversal-gates section is in the latter part of the manuscript before the Discussion; I could not pin it to exact printed pages 8-9 from the HTML, but it exists and contains exactly this content, and Tables 1-2 ("Optimal weight-8 self-dual BB codes" for n<=110 and 110<n<=200) contain all five listed codes. (2) Per the paper, transversal S needs NO physical qubit permutation and transversal H is plain H^n with a logical X<->Z exchange (not a relabeling); so the claim's "up to logical permutation" phrasing is a slight overstatement for these specific gates — the logical action is the standard H/S/CNOT logical maps, not a nontrivial qubit permutation. (3) Year: arXiv preprint posted Oct 8, 2025, so "2025" is correct (the Error Correction Zoo lists "2026", likely a publication/citation-style date). None of these affect keep.

name: Quantum BCH non-FT |0_L> encoder (CNOT+H), cyclic-symmetric
paper: 'Efficient Fault-Tolerant Ancilla Preparation for Quantum BCH codes via Cyclic Symmetry'
figure_or_section: Appendix D 'Non-FT Circuit Layouts', Figures 13-15; cyclic C_n and Frobenius F_m automorphisms in Sec III / Table 1
availability: construct from figures (Appendix D gives explicit CNOT+H gate sequences); foundational code definitions from Grassl-Beth quantum BCH. Cyclic shift j->j+1 and Frobenius j->2j automorphisms generate permutation-equivalent encoder forms.
why_good_benchmark: Scalable structured family with EXPLICIT published unitary encoders, plus built-in cyclic/Frobenius automorphisms that map an encoder to a qubit-permuted equivalent encoder — directly a 'two equivalent forms' equivalence-check (circuit vs its automorphism image). High rate makes larger benchmark instances.
_subdomain: Encoder circuits for larger and structured CSS-family codes (quantum Reed-Muller, Hamming, BCH, Golay, polar, and QLDPC / bivariate-bicycle codes)
_availability_verdict: Construct from the paper plus tooling. The non-FT |0>^n preparation circuit is a Clifford (CNOT+H) encoder. Explicit gate-level figures are given in Appendix D "Non-FT Circuit Layouts" — Fig 13 ([[31,11,5]]), Fig 14 ([[63,27,7]]), Fig 15 ([[127,71,9]]). The [[255,159,13]] code is discussed in the abstract/conclusion but does NOT have an explicit non-FT circuit figure in Appendix D, so for n=255 you must synthesize the encoder yourself. Reliable route: use mqt.qecc (heuristic_encoding_circuit / gate_optimal_encoding_circuit / depth_optimal_encoding_circuit) on the Grassl-Beth quantum BCH parity-check matrices — this is the same tooling the paper used. Cyclic shift (j->j+1) and Frobenius (j->2j) automorphisms (Sec III, Thm 1, Table 1) then map any encoder to a qubit-permuted equivalent.
_correction: Minor scope correction on the codes/figures: Appendix D Figures 13-15 give explicit non-FT circuits only for [[31,11,5]], [[63,27,7]], and [[127,71,9]]. The [[255,159,13]] code is mentioned (abstract/conclusion, Appendix E performance tables) but has NO explicit non-FT circuit figure — it must be synthesized via mqt.qecc, not "read off from figures." So the "construct from figures" availability claim holds for n=31,63,127 but not n=255. Otherwise all claims verified: title exact, authors Kohei Yamamoto & Keisuke Fujii, arXiv 2605.19471 (submitted May 2026 — the id 2605 = year 2026 month 05, consistent), Appendix D title "Non-FT Circuit Layouts" correct, Sec III Theorem 1 Aut(BCH) ⊇ C_n ⋊ F_m with Frobenius j->2j correct, Table 1 exists. The non-FT preparation circuit is genuinely purely unitary (CNOT+H, no measurement/reset); measurements appear only in the SEPARATE downstream entanglement-distillation stage, which is correctly excluded from this entry.

name: General CSS unitary encoder for QLDPC codes (row-echelon / CNOT+H)
code: Any CSS QLDPC code (hypergraph product, bivariate bicycle, etc.); demonstrated on small [[9,4,2]]/[[9,6,2]]-derived examples
purely_unitary: yes (the non-fault-tolerant encoder of Sec III)
paper: Sharma, Garani, 'Fault-Tolerant Quantum LDPC Encoders'
figure_or_section: Sec III 'Non-fault-tolerant Encoders' (E = D-dagger, D = W T U from row-reducing H_CSS); Fig 1; the FT version (Sec IV, Fig 3) is entanglement-assisted and not purely unitary
availability: construct from algorithm: reduce H_CSS to row-echelon form, then U=CNOTs (X-stabilizers), T=Hadamards, W=CNOTs (Z-stabilizers); encoder = D-dagger. Standard Cleve-Gottesman-style CSS encoder. Applies to bivariate-bicycle stabilizers.
why_good_benchmark: General-purpose unitary CSS encoder applicable to any structured/QLDPC code (including the gross code's stabilizers). The non-FT encoder and the entanglement-assisted FT encoder encode related states; the row/column-operation freedom yields many gate-equivalent encoder variants for the same code (per the paper, the CSS encoder is non-unique).
_subdomain: Encoder circuits for larger and structured CSS-family codes (quantum Reed-Muller, Hamming, BCH, Golay, polar, and QLDPC / bivariate-bicycle codes)
_availability_verdict: Confirmed: construct from the Sec III algorithm. Row-reduce H_CSS to row-echelon form (eq. 3), then D = W T U with U = CNOTs zeroing the X-stabilizer A^(x)|B^(x) block, T = Hadamards on the first rho1 qubits, W = CNOTs (from solving A^(z)P = B^(z)) zeroing the B^(z) block; the encoder is E = D-dagger. Gate set is exactly {CNOT, H}, no measurement/reset. This is a standard Cleve-Gottesman-style CSS encoder and is reproducible directly from the paper. No public code repository is referenced; build it yourself from the algorithm. Note the paper itself only demonstrates the EA examples [[9,4,2;1]] -> [[15,10,2;1]], not any bivariate-bicycle/gross-code instance.
_correction: Three corrections. (1) FIGURE NUMBER WRONG: the non-FT encoder is Figure 2(a), NOT Figure 1. Figure 1 is the CNOT error-propagation model. The FT/EA encoder is Figure 3. Section III reference is correct. (2) EXAMPLE DIMENSIONS WRONG: the paper's worked example is the entanglement-assisted [[9,4,2;1]] code extended to [[15,10,2;1]] (note the ';1' = 1 EPR pair). There is NO [[9,6,2]] anywhere; even the [[9,4,2;1]] base is EA, not an unassisted code. (3) BIVARIATE BICYCLE / GROSS CODE NOT IN PAPER: the words 'bivariate', 'bicycle', and 'gross code' do not appear; tying this construction to BB stabilizers or the gross code is the finder's extrapolation, not sourced. The algorithm is generic CSS row-reduction and could in principle apply to any CSS/QLDPC code, but the paper neither states nor demonstrates this for BB codes. Minor nuance: the FT (Sec IV/Fig 3) encoder's gates are still CNOT/H, but it relies on preshared entanglement (EPR pairs), so the finder's 'not purely unitary' label on the FT version is reasonable.

name: Quantum Hamming code [[2^r-1, 2^r-2r-1, 3]] CSS encoder
code: Quantum Hamming family [[2^r-1, 2^r-2r-1, 3]]; smallest member is the [[7,1,3]] Steane code; e.g. [[15,7,3]], [[31,21,3]]
paper: errorcorrectionzoo entry (CSS construction from Hamming/simplex codes); concatenation analysis in recent decoding work
figure_or_section: errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/quantum_hamming_css (H_X = H_Z = simplex generator matrix, weight-2^{r-1} stabilizers); decoding paper arXiv:2601.09131
availability: construct from stabilizers: apply standard CSS Gaussian-elimination encoder (CNOT+H) to the simplex generator H_X=H_Z. No single canonical figure; the [[7,1,3]] member's encoder is the well-known Steane encoder (gold-standard reference Chamberland-Cross 1811.00566 Fig 4).
why_good_benchmark: Scalable CSS family generalizing the gold-standard Steane code; the [[7,1,3]] member gives a direct tie-in to the existing benchmark, and larger members provide bigger instances. Encoder non-uniqueness (row/column ops) yields equivalent variants. Caveat: arXiv id for the explicit general-r unitary encoder is unverified — mark availability as construct-from-definition.
_subdomain: Encoder circuits for larger and structured CSS-family codes (quantum Reed-Muller, Hamming, BCH, Golay, polar, and QLDPC / bivariate-bicycle codes)
_availability_verdict: Construct-from-definition is correct and is the only honest route: the code family is real (errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/quantum_hamming_css, VERIFIED — self-dual CSS from Hamming/simplex codes, H_X=H_Z=simplex generator, weight-2^(r-1) stabilizers, lists [[7,1,3]] Steane and [[15,7,3]] as members), but that page contains NO encoder circuit or figure. There is NO published canonical general-r unitary encoder figure; apply the standard CSS Gaussian-elimination encoder (CNOT+H) to H_X=H_Z. The decoding paper arXiv:2601.09131 is REAL ('Bidirectional Decoding for Concatenated Quantum Hamming Codes', Zhang/Wu/Wu/Huang, 2026-01-14) but is purely a DECODER paper — it provides no encoder and references only [[15,7,3]] as a test case, not [[31,21,3]]. For the [[7,1,3]] member, a concrete unitary circuit exists locally at /Users/stavanjain/Code/QECUnitaryCircuits/steane_H_nf_fig4.qasm (7 qubits, only H/T/S/CX, no measurement/reset), but see correction: that circuit is the Fig-4 magic-state prep, not a generic encoder.
_correction: CITATION MISMATCH on the [[7,1,3]] anchor. The claim states 'the [[7,1,3]] member's encoder is the well-known Steane encoder (gold-standard reference Chamberland-Cross 1811.00566 Fig 4).' I read the actual PDF: Figure 4 of arXiv:1811.00566 is captioned 'Non-fault-tolerant circuit for preparing a logical |H> state encoded in the [[7,1,3]] Steane code ... if there are no faults then |H>_nf = T-bar|0-bar>.' This is a MAGIC-STATE (|H>) PREPARATION circuit producing T-bar|0-bar> using an |H> resource state and Clifford+T gates — it is NOT a generic Steane logical-qubit encoder (it prepares one specific logical state, not an arbitrary input). The local steane_H_nf_fig4.qasm confirms this (h;t;h;s on q[2] prepares the |H> resource). So Fig 4 should be described as Steane |H> magic-state prep, not 'the Steane encoder.' SECOND: the 'scalable general-r CNOT+H unitary encoder' has no verified source — purely_unitary='yes' holds only as construct-from-definition (standard CSS encoder is genuinely unitary), so the blanket 'yes' should be softened to 'yes (construct-from-definition; no cited general-r circuit).' THIRD: arXiv:2601.09131 does not contain encoder circuits and references [[15,7,3]], not [[31,21,3]] — drop the implication that the decoding paper supplies the encoder.

name: Surface code state-expanding unitary encoder: Rot(3)->Rot(5) via local vs non-local circuit
code: rotated surface code, Rot(3)->Rot(5) (and general Rot(d)->Rot(2d-1))
purely_unitary: yes (explicitly a unitary circuit, no measurement during expansion)
paper: A Unitary Encoder for Surface Codes
figure_or_section: Fig. 2 (local reference encoder Rot(3)->Rot(5)), Fig. 3 (new non-local encoder), Fig. 8 Appendix A (initial Rot(3) circuit), Appendix E (gate counts 6d^2-10d+4)
availability: construct from figure (Figs. 2,3,8); pure-CX circuits are easy to transcribe to OpenQASM 2.0
why_good_benchmark: The paper's whole point is two equivalent unitary encoders preparing the same code state: a local MERA-style encoder (depth ~7 log d) vs the new non-local encoder (depth ~4 log d) that 'halves the gate count'. Ideal optimized-vs-baseline equivalence check; CX-only keeps it Clifford and clean.
_subdomain: Surface code and color code purely-unitary operations (encoders / logical-state-prep, transversal and fold-transversal logical Clifford gates, unitary equivalents of lattice surgery)
_availability_verdict: Construct from figures in arXiv:2506.04084 (Fig. 2 = local CX-only Rot(3)->Rot(5) encoder; Fig. 3 = non-local Rot(d)->Reg(d)->Rot(2d-1) encoder; Fig. 8/Appendix A = initial Rot(3) base-case circuit). No public OpenQASM/GitHub release was found, so it must be transcribed by hand from the figures. IMPORTANT caveat for transcription: the expansion stages (Figs. 2 and 3) are pure-CX and trivially convertible to OpenQASM 2.0, but a complete encoder from |0...0> also requires the initial Rot(3) preparation (Appendix A), which uses single-qubit gates (an H_YZ / TH-type gate) to make |+i_L>. So "pure-CX" is exact only for the expansion sub-circuit, not the full from-scratch encoder.
_correction: Two corrections. (1) Depth scaling is misattributed: the paper's non-local encoder is 4*log2(d)+O(1); the "7*log2(d)+O(1)" figure is the PREVIOUS fastest (MERA-based) encoder it is compared against, NOT this paper's local reference encoder. This paper's own local encoder (Fig. 2) has depth 2d+O(1) (linear), not "~7 log d". So the framing "local ~7 log d vs non-local ~4 log d" is wrong; it is really "prior MERA 7 log d vs this paper's non-local 4 log d", with the in-paper local reference being linear-depth 2d. (2) "Gate set: CNOT only" is accurate for the expansion circuits (Figs. 2 and 3) but NOT for the full from-scratch encoder: the base-case Rot(3) preparation in Appendix A/Fig. 8 needs single-qubit gates (H_YZ/TH) to prepare |+i_L>. Minor: the gate-count formula 6d^2-10d+4 is correctly cited as the non-local Rot(d)->Rot(2d-1) cost (Appendix E); the per-step local cost is 8d+4 CX for Rot(d)->Rot(d+2). Qubit count (25 for Rot(5), d^2 general) and the Rot(3)->Rot(5) / Rot(d)->Rot(2d-1) expansion are all confirmed.

name: Rotated surface code d=3 (Surface-17) |0>_L unitary encoder (RL-discovered, local)
num_qubits: 9 data qubits (no ancilla variant); a stabilizer-expanding variant uses up to 17 with ancillas
gate_set: H, CNOT (CX); |0>_L needs only CX after |+> inits, |+>_L variant symmetric
paper: Zen et al., 'Unitary fault-tolerant encoding of Pauli states in surface codes' (generalizes the RL-discovered surface-17 encoder)
figure_or_section: Fig. 2 (XX/ZZ stabilizer-expanding building blocks), Fig. 3 and Fig. 4 (full d=5 encoder, 22 then 13 timesteps), Appendix A Figs. 6-8 (|+>_L); d=3 surface-17 is the seed circuit
availability: construct from figure (Fig. 2-4); also generatable via stim Tableau.from_stabilizers(...).to_circuit() or qiskit synth_circuit_from_stabilizers from the d=3 rotated-surface stabilizer generators, then export to OpenQASM 2.0 via qiskit
why_good_benchmark: Two equivalent unitary encoders for the same logical state: the geometrically-local d=O(d)-depth version (Fig. 3) vs the reduced-timestep intra-plaquette version (Fig. 4, 22->13 steps). Equivalence-checking the optimized vs unoptimized encoder is exactly the target use case; also small (9 qubits) so tractable.
_subdomain: Surface code and color code purely-unitary operations (encoders / logical-state-prep, transversal and fold-transversal logical Clifford gates, unitary equivalents of lattice surgery)
_availability_verdict: Two valid routes, both confirmed. (1) Construct from the paper's figures: the geometrically-local building blocks (Fig. 2, XX stabilizer expansion) compose into the |0>_L encoder; the full worked encoders shown are d=5 (Fig. 3 with ancillas / Fig. 4 without), and d=3 UNROTATED (Figs. 7-8, the |+>_L appendix). The d=3 rotated surface-17 encoder is the RL-discovered seed/inspiration (Zen et al. 2025, Ref. 41) and is reconstructed from the same building blocks rather than printed as its own d=3 rotated figure. (2) Auto-generate from the d=3 rotated-surface stabilizer generators: qiskit.synthesis.synth_circuit_from_stabilizers(...) is a real function returning a unitary state-prep circuit (Gaussian elimination over Clifford gates); stim.Tableau.from_stabilizers(...).to_circuit() also exists and returns a unitary Clifford decomposition (default graph-state method, gates H/S/CX). Both auto routes give A unitary encoder for the same logical state but NOT the geometrically-local RL circuit and NOT a CX-only circuit. Export to OpenQASM via qiskit is valid.
_correction: Minor corrections, none disqualifying. (1) The paper does not print a standalone "d=3 rotated surface-17" figure; the labeled full-encoder figures are d=5 (Figs. 3/4) and d=3 UNROTATED (Figs. 7/8 in Appendix A). The d=3 rotated surface-17 is the RL seed (cited as Zen et al. 2025, Ref. 41) reconstructed from the Fig. 2 building blocks. The claim's "Fig. 2 ... ; d=3 surface-17 is the seed circuit" is accurate as inspiration but should not be read as a printed d=3 rotated figure. (2) Appendix-A timesteps are 11->7 for the d=3 (unrotated) |+>_L encoder; the 22->13 figures (Figs. 3/4) are d=5, which the claim states correctly. (3) Gate-set nuance: |0>_L needs H (to prepare |+> pivots) plus CX, not literally CX-only; the paper inits all qubits in |0> then uses pivot |+> + CX. (4) Auto-generation API: stim's to_circuit yields H/S/CX (not CX-only) and is a different, non-local encoder than the figure circuits; the qiskit/stim routes give an equivalent-logical-state unitary encoder, useful for the stated equivalence-checking benchmark but not byte-identical to the RL-local circuit. The "17 with ancillas" framing is loose: surface-17 = 9 data + 8 syndrome ancillas; this paper's unitary encoder uses at most one transient connectivity ancilla per stabilizer (never measured), not the full 8-ancilla syndrome-extraction layout.

name: [[23,1,7]] Golay code logical-zero state-prep (depth-7, 57 CNOTs) and optimized 56-gate variant
_availability_verdict: Confirmed. The depth-7, 57-CNOT |0_L> encoder is given explicitly as Figure 4 of Paetznick-Reichardt arXiv:1106.2190 v2 (Section 3.2, "Overlap method"); it can be transcribed directly from the paper (verbatim caption: "57 CNOT gates applied in seven rounds"). The Golay code is self-dual, so |+_L> is the dual circuit (swap roles). The 56-entangling-gate variant is obtainable from Webster, Koutsioumpas & Browne arXiv:2503.14660 and its code repository github.com/m-webster/CliffordOpt (the paper states logical-state-prep results are in their GitHub repo). The full fault-tolerant preparation (Fig. 3, ~297 CNOTs, four verified ancillas) adds X-error verification measurements/post-selection; extract only the bare Figure-4 encoder for a purely-unitary circuit.
_correction: The "56-gate" variant is misattributed. It was NOT found by reinforcement learning. It was found by the A* search method of Webster, Koutsioumpas & Browne, arXiv:2503.14660 ("Heuristic and Optimal Synthesis of CNOT and Clifford Circuits", 18 Mar 2025): the paper states "Using our A* method, we found an encoding circuit using 56 entangling two qubit gates, improving the best-known result." The reinforcement-learning work (Zen, Olle, Colmenarez, Puviani, Mueller, Marquardt, arXiv:2402.17761, PRX 2025) actually reported 61 entangling gates for the Golay |0_L> circuit, not 56. So: 77 (Latin-rectangle, Steane) -> 57 (Paetznick-Reichardt overlap, depth-7) -> 61 (RL/Zen et al.) -> 56 (A*/Webster et al., current best). The claim's listed arXiv 2307.14425 ("code conversion with the quantum Golay code") is a real paper but is NOT the source of the 56-gate circuit and is only tangential context. Correct source arXiv ids: 1106.2190 (57-CNOT depth-7) and 2503.14660 (56-gate). Minor: Paetznick-Reichardt note Fig. 4 "may not be optimal... there are equivalent circuits with fewer CNOT gates" but is "the smallest circuit we found that also preserves depth" -- consistent with the later 56-gate improvement. Everything else (depth-7, 57 CNOTs, CNOT+H gate set, not-FT-alone bare encoder, transversal single-qubit Clifford on all 23 qubits) is confirmed verbatim.

name: Logical Clifford gates from code automorphisms (gross [[144,12,12]] BB code and others)
code: Bivariate bicycle gross [[144,12,12]]; also [[5,1,3]], [[4,2,2]], [[7,1,3]], [[17,1,7]], best-known-distance codes
gate_set: SWAP + single-qubit Cliffords H, S, sqrt(X); decomposed in 6 layers H-CNOT-S-CZ-X-C(X,X)
paper: Sayginel, Koutsioumpas, Webster, Rajput, Browne, 'Fault-Tolerant Logical Clifford Gates from Code Automorphisms'
figure_or_section: Sec VIII examples (VIII.1 [[5,1,3]] Fig 2; VIII.4 bivariate bicycle); Sec V-VI (symplectic-matrix-to-circuit); algorithm outline figure
availability: generate via library: GitHub https://github.com/hsayginel/autqec (pip install autqec). Verified locally: example notebook examples/bivariate_bicycle_codes/[[144,12,12]].ipynb loads H_X/H_Z, computes automorphisms, builds physical Clifford circuit via circ_from_aut(...).circ(), computes logical action via logical_circ_and_pauli_correct(...).U_logical_act(), exports to qiskit (construct_circuit). MAGMA optional (Bliss/igraph fallback).
why_good_benchmark: Generates a PURELY UNITARY physical Clifford circuit (permutation + single-qubit Cliffords) AND its computed symplectic logical action. Perfect equivalence-checking targets: (1) physical automorphism circuit vs the abstract logical Clifford it implements; (2) two different automorphisms realizing the same logical gate give equivalent circuits. Covers the gross [[144,12,12]] code directly and a ladder of small codes.
_subdomain: Encoder circuits for larger and structured CSS-family codes (quantum Reed-Muller, Hamming, BCH, Golay, polar, and QLDPC / bivariate-bicycle codes)
_availability_verdict: Confirmed. `pip install autqec` (PyPI) and GitHub hsayginel/autqec both exist. The exact notebook examples/bivariate_bicycle_codes/[[144,12,12]].ipynb exists in the repo, along with [[5,1,3]].ipynb, [[4,2,2]].ipynb, [[7,1,3]].ipynb, [[17,1,7]].ipynb and code_data (HX/HZ .npy) for the [[144,12,12]] gross code. Functions circ_from_aut, .circ() (returns 1-qubit Cliffords + SWAPs), logical_circ_and_pauli_correct, U_logical_act() are all defined in autqec/automorphisms.py. MAGMA is optional as of v1.0.0 with a Bliss graph-automorphism fallback. CORRECTION: construct_circuit lives in autqec/utils/qiskit.py (not automorphisms.py); it imports qiskit and builds a QuantumCircuit, confirming qiskit export. I did not execute the notebook (autqec not installed in this env), so the claimant's 'verified locally' is unconfirmed by me, but every artifact/function it cites was independently verified to exist in the repo source.
_correction: Two minor imprecisions, neither fatal: (1) construct_circuit (the qiskit export) is in autqec/utils/qiskit.py, not automorphisms.py as the availability text implies. (2) The exact '6 layers H-CNOT-S-CZ-X-C(X,X)' order could not be verbatim-confirmed from the paper's rendered text (Appendix B content was not exposed), but it is strongly corroborated by autqec/utils/symplectic.py, which defines exactly these primitives: H_gate, CNOT_gate, S_gate, CZ_gate, Pauli X, and CX_XX_gate (=C(X,X), built as rxx(pi/2)), plus Xsqrt_gate and SWAP_gate matching the stated gate set. The [[144,12,12]] gross code, [[5,1,3]], [[4,2,2]], [[7,1,3]], [[17,1,7]] are all genuinely present.

name: Steane / 2D color code [[7,1,3]] non-fault-tolerant |H>_L encoder (gold-standard reference)
paper: Chamberland & Cross, 'Fault-tolerant magic state preparation with flag qubits'
figure_or_section: Figure 4
availability: existing QASM repo: already present at /Users/stavanjain/Code/QECUnitaryCircuits/steane_H_nf_fig4.qasm (7 qubits, H/S/T/CNOT, no measurement)
why_good_benchmark: The provided gold-standard; the standard 9-CNOT + 4-H Steane encoder (errorcorrectionzoo) gives an alternate equivalent encoding circuit, so an optimized/standard equivalence pair exists. Self-dual CSS structure makes alternate constructions abundant.
_subdomain: Surface code and color code purely-unitary operations (encoders / logical-state-prep, transversal and fold-transversal logical Clifford gates, unitary equivalents of lattice surgery)
_availability_verdict: Confirmed. The file exists at /Users/stavanjain/Code/QECUnitaryCircuits/steane_H_nf_fig4.qasm: 7 qubits (qreg q[7]), 18 gate operations using only H, S, T, CNOT, and zero measurement/reset/classical-control instructions. I numerically simulated the QASM and confirmed it produces the logical |H> magic state of the [[7,1,3]] Steane code (see correction field for evidence). The standard alternate encoder (errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/steane) cites "nine CNOT and four Hadamard gates" [Nielsen-Chuang Fig 10.14], confirming the claimed equivalence-pair rationale.
_correction: none. All claims verified. Citation is real: Chamberland & Cross, "Fault-tolerant magic state preparation with flag qubits," arXiv:1811.00566, Quantum 3, 143 (2019). Figure 4 caption (from arXiv HTML, ar5iv) reads: "Non-fault-tolerant circuit for preparing a logical |H> state encoded in the [[7,1,3]] Steane code." — exactly matching the claim, and it is a unitary-only (gates: H, S, T, CNOT) preparation circuit, NOT a syndrome-measurement round (those are Figs 3 and 5, which DO use flag/ancilla measurements). Independent numerical verification of the local QASM: the output state has +1 expectation for all six standard Steane stabilizers (IIIXXXX, IXXIIXX, XIXIXIX, IIIZZZZ, IZZIIZZ, ZIZIZIZ = +1.0000 each), is a +1 eigenstate of the transversal logical Hadamard H_L = H^⊗7 (<H_L> = 1.0000), and has <X_L> = <Z_L> = 0.7071 = 1/sqrt(2), matching the magic state |H> = cos(pi/8)|0> + sin(pi/8)|1>. So it is genuinely the logical |H>_L state, 7 qubits, purely unitary. Minor terminology note (not an error in the entry): the [[7,1,3]] Steane code is indeed the smallest 2D color code, so "Steane / 2D color code" is accurate.

name: Steane [[7,1,3]] standard encoding circuit (9 CNOT + 4 H)
paper: Error Correction Zoo Steane-code entry (standard textbook encoder, after Steane 1996; Goto minimal-overhead variants)
figure_or_section: Error Correction Zoo c/steane 'Encoding' section (9 CNOT, 4 H); cf. Goto, Sci. Rep. 6, 19578 (2016) for minimized variant
availability: generate via library (qiskit synth_circuit_from_stabilizers / stim Tableau.from_stabilizers from the 6 Steane stabilizers + logical Z) then OpenQASM export; also widely available as hand-written QASM in tutorials (e.g. NordIQuEst CSS Steane tutorial)
why_good_benchmark: Canonical |0>_L encoder; pairs directly with the Chamberland-Cross Fig.4 prep and with Goto's resource-minimized encoder, giving multiple equivalent constructions of the same logical state. Small and CSS.
_subdomain: Surface code and color code purely-unitary operations (encoders / logical-state-prep, transversal and fold-transversal logical Clifford gates, unitary equivalents of lattice surgery)
_availability_verdict: Correct in substance. The purely-unitary encoder is reliably generated from the 6 Steane stabilizers (+ logical Z) via qiskit.synthesis.synth_circuit_from_stabilizers (real; outputs a Clifford H/CNOT/S unitary) or stim.Tableau.from_stabilizers followed by Tableau.to_circuit (both real, from_stabilizers added in stim v1.13.0, Mar 2024), then OpenQASM-exported. The canonical 9-CNOT + 4-H form is also documented verbatim in the Error Correction Zoo c/steane 'Encoding' section. CAVEAT on the NordIQuEst/ENCCS CSS-Steane tutorial cited in 'availability': it does NOT provide a purely-unitary 9-CNOT+4-H encoder — it implements Goto's measurement-based scheme (8 CNOT encode + 3 CNOT for Z_L measurement + ancilla measurement + reset/repeat). Use the library-synthesis route (or the Zoo/Nakahara hand-written circuit) for a genuinely unitary version.
_correction: Citation attribution is imprecise. The Error Correction Zoo c/steane 'Encoding' section does state '9 CNOT and 4 Hadamard gates' (claim verified), but it attributes this to Nakahara's 2008 textbook (ref [10], Fig. 10.14) — NOT to 'Steane 1996' and NOT to Goto. Recommend updating 'paper'/'figure_or_section' to credit Nakahara 2008 Fig. 10.14 (via the Zoo) for the 9-CNOT+4-H figure. Second, the cited Goto 'minimal-overhead variant' (Sci. Rep. 6, 19578 (2016)) is NOT purely unitary: it is a non-FT encoding circuit FOLLOWED BY verification via stabilizer measurement with post-selection and restart-on-flag; only its encoding sub-block is unitary. It is fine as a 'related construction' reference but should not be presented as an equivalent purely-unitary encoder. Same caveat applies to the NordIQuEst tutorial cited under availability (it is the measurement-based Goto scheme). Year 1996 is reasonable for the conceptual origin (Steane), though the specific 9+4 figure traces to Nakahara 2008.

name: Rotated surface code d=5 |0>_L unitary encoder (local vs reduced-depth)
num_qubits: 25 data qubits (ancilla-mediated variant adds syndrome qubits)
paper: Zen et al., 'Unitary fault-tolerant encoding of Pauli states in surface codes'
figure_or_section: Fig. 3 (22-step ancilla-mediated d=5 encoder) and Fig. 4 (13-step intra-plaquette d=5 encoder)
availability: construct from figure (Figs. 3-4); or generate via stim/qiskit stabilizer-to-circuit synthesis from d=5 rotated-surface stabilizers and export OpenQASM
why_good_benchmark: Same logical |0>_L prepared by two explicitly different unitary circuits (22 vs 13 timesteps) in the same paper - a direct optimized/unoptimized equivalence pair at a non-trivial size (25 qubits).
_subdomain: Surface code and color code purely-unitary operations (encoders / logical-state-prep, transversal and fold-transversal logical Clifford gates, unitary equivalents of lattice surgery)
_availability_verdict: Confirmed. Two routes work. (1) Reconstruct directly from Fig. 3 (ancilla-mediated, d=5) and Fig. 4 (intra-plaquette, d=5) of arXiv:2601.05113 — H + CNOT only, with step-1 |0>/|+> initialization.
